text: Extra-levator abdominoperoneal resection should be considered for patients with low rectal tumour where distal margin clearance is not possible or if the tumour is invading the anal sphincters. Sometimes it should also be considered for patients with very poor sphincter control who may not tolerate a coloanal anastomosis. Once the patient is anaesthetised, the patient must be carefully positioned in a Lloyd Davis position for the abdominal part of the operation to allow access to the pelvis. Before starting the procedure, an examination under anaesthesia should be performed by the surgeon to reassess and confirm the level of tumour and sphincter involvement.

The surgeon makes a low midline incision and performs a laparotomy to explore the abdomen for metastatic disease, examining the liver, peritoneal cavity, and pelvis. The sigmoid colon is mobilised by incising the line of Toldt, with the ureters identified and protected at the pelvic brim. The peritoneum is incised, and the rectum is mobilised down the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane to the coccyx posteriorly, while preserving the pelvic nerves. Anteriorly, the Denonvilliers fascia is developed to just below the seminal vesicles in men and the vagina in women. The inferior mesenteric vessels are ligated and divided, and the sigmoid colon is divided.

A medium swab is sutured to the proximal end of the specimen and placed in the presacral space in front of the coccyx. A left iliac fossa colostomy is formed at the premarked site, ensuring a well-vascularized and adequately mobilized length of proximal colon. The abdomen is closed, completing the abdominal part of the operation.

Dressings are applied to the surgical wounds, and gelonet and gauze are placed over the stoma for protection. The patient is positioned in the prone jackknife position with legs slightly abducted, and pressure areas are protected. The buttocks are taped apart for improved exposure. The anus is closed with a double purse string suture. The coccyx and sacrococcygeal joint are palpated and marked. A teardrop skin incision is made around the anus, outside the sphincter complex, extending just beyond the sacrococcygeal joint. Dissection is performed close to the external anal sphincter, avoiding the fat envelope of the ischiorectal fossa unless there is involvement of the external sphincter.

The surgeon uses a lone star retractor to enhance exposure. The resection plane begins along the external sphincter, extending laterally to the levator ani muscle and its insertion into the obturator muscle on the pelvic side wall. Posterior dissection proceeds towards the coccyx and sacrococcygeal joint, with the option to disarticulate the coccyx at the joint level or leave it intact.

The surgeon divides the fascia to enter the peritoneal cavity, retrieving the medium swab placed in the presacral space. The levator muscles are divided to create a large opening for specimen delivery. The cylindrical specimen shows the levator muscles wrapped around the lower rectum. Anterior dissection of the rectum off the prostate or vaginal vault is performed under direct vision, with care taken to avoid urethral injury. Argon is used for haemostasis. The pelvic floor defect is reconstructed using a 10x10 cm Biodesign biological mesh.

The surgeon rehydrates a biological mesh in 0.9% saline for less than one minute before securing it to the cut edge of the pelvic floor using interrupted Proline or PDS sutures. The mesh is fixed in place, and a suction drain is positioned over it. The tape holding the buttocks apart is released, and the fat is approximated in layers with interrupted absorbable sutures to minimize dead space. Finally, mattress silk sutures are used to approximate the skin, and the patient is transferred to a post-operative bed.

The surgeon must review the stoma to ensure that it is still viable before the patient is woken up by the anaesthetist. We recommend that the patient is nursed on their side for the first forty-eight hours and encouraged to mobilise without sitting on their perineum for extended periods within the first week.
